coredata.dc:title: Enhanced performance with ionic and organic redox-couple electrolytes on MTMO anchored CQD nanocomposites and renewable carbon-based asymmetric flexible supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Sinha, Prerna
Kar, Kamal K.
coredata.dc:description: The work utilizes a hybrid approach at electrode and electrolyte systems to fabricate a high-energy density supercapacitor. Hybrid nanocomposite electrodes were fabricated using a facile hydrothermal synthesis. The kinetics were studied by preparing in-situ and ex-situ nanocomposites. The in-situ preparation delivers a homogeneous flower petal-like interconnected porous morphology with an appreciable BET surface area of 97 m2 g−1. The actual electrochemical performance was analyzed by accounting for discharge area instead of discharge time to avoid discrepancies related to hybrid energy storage systems. The three-electrode measurement suggests that the electrode delivers an excellent specific capacitance of 3802 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. In the asymmetric configuration, the device reveals excellent actual specific capacitance of 292 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. However, upon adding 0.02 M KI as redox mediators in a traditional electrolyte (6 M KOH), a ∼37% increase in specific capacitance is achieved without the degradation in the device stability. The nanoarchitecture porous morphology and modification in traditional electrolyte enables a high-performance device having an energy density of 165 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1, which retain till 50 Wh kg−1 at 25 kW kg−1. The nanocomposite also shows excellent compatibility in a flexible device assembly, delivering a specific capacitance of 192 mF cm−1 at 1 mA cm−1 without deteriorating its performance upon different bending angles. The study claims the design of a low-cost, environmentally benign, and facile approach for developing a high-performance hybrid supercapacitor.

head.ce:title: Enhanced performance with ionic and organic redox-couple electrolytes on MTMO anchored CQD nanocomposites and renewable carbon-based asymmetric flexible supercapacitor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work utilizes a hybrid approach at electrode and electrolyte systems to fabricate a high-energy density supercapacitor. Hybrid nanocomposite electrodes were fabricated using a facile hydrothermal synthesis. The kinetics were studied by preparing in-situ and ex-situ nanocomposites. The in-situ preparation delivers a homogeneous flower petal-like interconnected porous morphology with an appreciable BET surface area of 97 m2 g−1. The actual electrochemical performance was analyzed by accounting for discharge area instead of discharge time to avoid discrepancies related to hybrid energy storage systems. The three-electrode measurement suggests that the electrode delivers an excellent specific capacitance of 3802 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. In the asymmetric configuration, the device reveals excellent actual specific capacitance of 292 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. However, upon adding 0.02 M KI as redox mediators in a traditional electrolyte (6 M KOH), a ∼37% increase in specific capacitance is achieved without the degradation in the device stability. The nanoarchitecture porous morphology and modification in traditional electrolyte enables a high-performance device having an energy density of 165 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1, which retain till 50 Wh kg−1 at 25 kW kg−1. The nanocomposite also shows excellent compatibility in a flexible device assembly, delivering a specific capacitance of 192 mF cm−1 at 1 mA cm−1 without deteriorating its performance upon different bending angles. The study claims the design of a low-cost, environmentally benign, and facile approach for developing a high-performance hybrid supercapacitor.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors are excellent energy storage devices that have gained significant attention due to their remarkable behavior. Its effortless charge-discharge characteristics, non-toxicity, wide operating temperature, and chemical inertness have made it stand out in the battery world. However, the comparatively poor energy density of supercapacitors continues to be a barrier to their widespread commercialization. Expansion of electrochemical stable potential window and development of high specific capacitance electrodes are the effective strategy to improve the energy density, needing optimum studies [1].

In supercapacitors, the charges are stored either electrostatically or Faradiacally. The classical electrode materials are carbons, including activated carbon, graphene, carbon nanotubes, metal oxides, metal hydroxides, metal sulfides, and conducting polymers. Carbon undergoes an electrostatic type charge storage process involving no electron transfer. The physisorption of electrolytic ions stores the charges at the electrode surface. The charge storage process depends on surface availability, so the carbon electrode shows poor capacitance [2,3]. However, carbon electrodes deliver excellent conductivity, high rate capability, and outstanding cyclic stability [4]. Metal oxides/sulfides/hydroxides offer pseudocapacitance, where electron transfer provides a larger capacitance value than carbon materials. The metal ions with variable oxidation states are preferred that can readily lose or gain electrons by changing their valence state. In pseudocapacitance, chemical processes are involved. Hence, the electrode performance deteriorates after a few cycles. Also, metal oxides have poor conductivity hindering the electron transfer pathway, low operational voltage, thereby decreasing overall performance [5]. Individually these materials cannot fulfill the performance requirements of ideal electrode material. However, when combined in a composite form, the synergistic effect of two different electrodes can provide remarkable performance. However, extensive research has been carried out for designing hybrid electrode but the work fails to optimize the performance parameters [6]. One such example is a high capacitance hybrid electrode fails to maintain its rate capability at a high current loading. Hence, the charge storage process in the composites differs from the parent material, which requires optimization of various parameters. The studies precisely focuses on the synthesis procedure for composite and the choice of suitable parent electrode material that determines the electrode properties. Also, the bottom-up and top-down, i.e., in-situ or ex-situ preparation along with precursors ratio, strongly influences the composite properties [7].

In this vein, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have emerged as a new carbon material for electrode application. Earlier, carbon dots applications were limited to sensors and photoluminescence. It also draws attention due to its excellent fabrication yield, good solvent dispersion, and inexpensive manufacturing cost [8]. Contrary to other carbon materials, its surface-to-volume ratio is high, which helps to improve the specific performance. The CQD contains core carbon atoms with sp3 hybridization and edges carbon with sp2 hybridization [9]. The presence of a π-electron network functions as an electron acceptor/donor, improving the conducting pathway and providing a resistance-free bridge for electron transportation [10].

On the other hand, mixed transition metal oxide (MTMO) provides improved performance from the parent metal oxides. Combining two metal oxides improves conductivity compared to pristine metal oxides and offers multiple oxidation states that promote fast electron transfer enhancing rate capability [11]. One such MTMO is nickel cobalt oxide, used as an anodic material in supercapacitors and batteries. However, their volume change effect renders its charge-discharge performance after a few cycles by increasing the contact resistance that had not been solved yet [8]. The poor electrode stability and charge storage kinetic degrade the device's efficiency limiting its commercial utilization [12].

One effective strategy to eliminate the volume change effect is adding carbon nanoparticles throughout the nickel-cobalt matrix. However, literature is available stating the supercapacitor performance of nickel cobalt oxide, but until now, further optimization has yet to be achieved [13–15]. Past research has shown that NiCo2O4 and C-dots composite//HPC-based asymmetric devices can be reversible work in 0–1.5 V delivering specific capacitance of 138 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 [8]. Recently, another group carried out a symmetric device assembly of NiCo2O4 and C-dots composite delivering the specific capacitance of 529 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with a poor energy density of 74 Wh kg−1 at 500 W kg−1 [16]. The composites prepared in the literature report either show high energy density or high capacitance. Hence, a hybridization approach must be developed to address the limitations of the reported literature. A combination of strategies has been implemented throughout the study to develop a high-performance supercapacitor device. The present study utilizes a novel approach where in-situ and ex-situ nanocomposites were synthesized. The presence of CQD throughout Ni-Co oxide (NCO) delivers excellent electrochemical properties. The abundance of π-conjugation and edges available sites in CQD acts as nucleating sites for Ni-Co oxides enabling homogenized composite synthesis. The presence of CQD throughout NCO improved the electrode conductivity. It also provides mechanical support, undergoes capacitive charge storage, and improves composite conductivity [17]. It is worth noting that the ratio of CQD and NCO needs to be optimized to achieve optimum performance. As stated earlier, the synthesis procedure is vital in determining the composite properties. The current study focuses on the new combination of composite NCOCQD via a simple low-temperature hydrothermal synthesis technique. Different varieties of composites were prepared by in-situ and ex-situ procedures. The in-situ and ex-situ synthesis procedures provide an insight into the synthesis-dependent composite properties, which had not been carried out yet. The microscopy and physicochemical analysis suggest that the composite contains nanoarchitecture morphology with mesoporosity. Also, elemental analysis by XPS shows the presence of oxygen vacancies throughout the composite electrode, promoting fast charge storage kinetics.

Coming to the electrochemical study, a pure electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) electrode has a constant dV/dt and a linear charge-discharge (electrosorption) profile. However, a pseudocapacitor or hybrid system has a nonlinear charge-discharge curve with varying dV/dt with voltage. As a result, the usual linear charge-discharge curve expectation does not hold true for the hybrid system. The concave discharge curve may store more charges than the linear curve, whereas the convex discharge profile can have lesser, as depicted in Fig. S1a. Accurate performance metrics are required to eliminate the discrepancies. The present work employs a correction method that involves measuring pseudocapacitance contribution at various scan rates by calculating the true performance parameter using the area of the discharge curve. The three-electrode electrochemical studies reveal that in-situ prepared nanocomposite depicts a true specific capacitance of 3082 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. In a device configuration, introducing redox-mediated electrolyte KI improves the device capacitance to ∼37% compared to the conventional electrolyte while maintaining the device stability to 87% till 10,000 cycles. In a flexible assembly, the specific capacitance of 192 mF cm−1 is obtained with an energy density of 38 Wh kg−1 at 1000 W kg−1. The study delivers a facile, low-cost synthesis strategy to prepare high-performance composite electrodes for hybrid asymmetric supercapacitors.

Materials: Glucose (Glucon D, Zydus Wellness), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, Alfa Aesar), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2.6 H2O), Alfa Aesar), urea (CH4N2O, local market), citric acid (C6H8O7, lemon juice), electric conductive carbon cloth (Shanghai Lishuo Composite Material Technology Co., Ltd.), carbon black (Alfa Aesar), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Alfa Aesar), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Qualigens Fine Chemicals, India), potassium hydroxide (KOH, Alfa Aesar), hydroquinone, HQ (C6H6O2, Alfa Aesar), potassium iodide (KI, Loba chemicals), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Loba chemicals), and Nafion (5% w/w in water and 1-propanol, Alfa Aesar). Deionized water was employed throughout the investigation for the treatment processes.

Preparation of Ni-Co oxide (NCO): 0.167 g of Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, 0.333 g Co(NO3)2.6 H2O, 0.5 g urea were mixed in (15 ml ethanol +15 ml DI water) solution. The solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave with 100 ml and heated at 180°C for 10 h. The hydrothermally obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained solute was dried at 60 ᴼC for 24 h to remove water content.

Preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQD): 1 g glucose was mixed with 1 ml of lemon juice in (15 ml ethanol + 15 ml DI water) solution. The solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave with 100 ml and heated at 180°C for 10 h. The hydrothermally obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The solution was separated from the solute to get pure CQD.

Preparation of ex-situ composite material: The as-synthesized NCO and CQD were taken in 1:3, 1:1, and 3:1 ratios and mixed thoroughly. The slurry was dried overnight at 60 ᴼC to remove the water content. Then the solution was pyrolyzed at 500 ᴼC for 2 h in an N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 2 ᴼC min−1. Fig. 1 a shows the synthesis procedure for the preparation of ex-situ nanocomposites.

The obtained ex-situ composites were named as:
(1) E:HE-1 for NCO 25% + CQD 75%
(2) E:HE-2 for NCO 50% + CQD 50%
(3) E:HE-3 for NCO 75% + CQD 25%
Preparation of in-situ composite material: The precursor of the parent materials are taken in 1:3, 1:1, and 3:1 ratios.

For composite ratio 1NCO prercursors:3CQD precursors: 42 mg of Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, 83 mg Co(NO3)2.6 H2O, 125 mg urea, 750 mg glucose was mixed with 750 μl of lemon juice were mixed in (15 ml ethanol + 15 ml DI water) solution.

For composite ratio 1NCO prercursors:1CQD precursors: 83 mg of Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, 167 mg Co(NO3)2.6 H2O, 250 mg urea, 500 mg glucose was mixed with 500 μl of lemon juice were mixed in (15 ml ethanol + 15 ml DI water) solution.

For composite ratio 3NCO prercursors:1CQD precursors: 125 mg of Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, 250 mg Co(NO3)2.6 H2O, 375 mg urea, 250 mg glucose was mixed with 250 μl of lemon juice were mixed in (15 ml ethanol + 15 ml DI water) solution.

The as-synthesized composite solutions were pyrolyzed at 500°C for 2 h in an N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 2°C min−1. Fig. 1b shows the schematic for the synthesis procedure of in-situ nanocomposites.

The obtained in-situ composites were named as:
(4) I:HE-1 for NCO 25% + CQD 75%
(5) I:HE-2 for NCO 50% + CQD 50%
(6) I:HE-3 for NCO 75% + CQD 25%

ce:para.ce:italic: Preparation of activated carbon as negative electrode for asymmetric supercapacitor:

ce:section.ce:para: The waste human hair was collected from the barbershop. The washed and dried human hair were carbonized to 900°C for 1 h with an isothermal hold at 220°C for 1.5 h in a nitrogen atmosphere. After carbonization, the char was mixed with KOH in a 1:4 ratio. The slurry was activated at 900°C for 1 h with an isothermal hold at 220°C for 1.5 h in a nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained carbon was washed vigorously with 1 M HCl, and after that, deionized water to remove KOH residue. The resultant hierarchically porous carbon was named HPC.

ce:para.ce:italic: Assembly of a three-electrode setup for electrochemical analysis of active material:

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical studies were carried out using an electrochemical testing station (Autolab metrohm potentiostat) at room temperature. For the three-electrode assembly, carbon cloth was used as the current collector. Before use, carbon cloth was washed with DI water followed by acetone and kept for drying at 60°C for 24 h. The working electrode was prepared for the three-electrode system by mixing 80 wt% of the active material, 10 wt% of PVDF, and 10 wt% of carbon black in 1 mL DMF solvent. The prepared solution was drop cast on a carbon cloth current collector and kept in an oven at 60°C for drying. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The three-electrode test was carried out in a 6 M KOH electrolyte having a mass loading of 1 mg cm−2.

A two-electrode cell configuration setup was prepared by placing positive and negative electrode material on the two stainless steel plates, which act as a current collector. The electrodes were separated by a filter paper soaked in 6 M KOH electrolyte. The configuration was tightly pressed and secured with the screws to form a two-electrode supercapacitor device. The supplementary information section presents detailed electrochemical analysis methods and electrode active material ratio (M+/M-) estimation in fabricating asymmetric cells.

ce:para.ce:italic: Fabrication of redox-active electrolyte-based asymmetric supercapacitor:

ce:section.ce:para: A similar process was used to fabricate two different redox-active electrolyte-based devices (I:HE-3). For that, the filter paper was soaked in redox-active electrolytes a) (6 M KOH + 0.02 M HQ) and b) (6 M KOH + 0.02 M KI).

ce:para.ce:italic: Design of flexible asymmetric redox-active supercapacitor:

ce:section.ce:para: For the assembly of a redox-active asymmetric flexible device, carbon cloth thread was used as a current collector. Each electrode ink (a) HPC and (b) I:HE-3 were prepared by mixing finely ground electrodes in a Nafion solution. The electrode ink was then dropped cast onto the carbon cloth thread and kept for drying for 60°C for 6 h. Five times, the process was repeated to coat the electrode ink onto the carbon thread evenly. The mass loading of 5 mg was taken to coat the fiber length of 5 cm. A redox-active gel electrolyte was synthesized using (1 g PVA+1 g KOH +0.1 g KI). The electrode-coated current collector was dipped in the redox-active gel electrolyte. The carbon threads were assembled in a parallel configuration, secured, and sealed with Teflon tape.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and thermal behavior of NCO and CQD hybrid electrode composite
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal technique is a low-cost synthesis mechanism that yields high reproducibility of the synthesized material. The synthesis was carried out at a temperature of 180°C for 10 h. In this technique, water accelerates the reaction by hydrolysis mechanism [18]. During NCO synthesis, urea is used as a catalyst. The carbonyl and amino groups in urea make it a highly active compound that acts as a salt bridge, accelerating the dissolution of the precursors' salts to synthesize NCO [19]. On the other hand, CQD has been prepared using bio-based glucose. The photoluminescence behavior of NCO nanowires and CQD can be seen in Fig. 1c, where both the particles glow in UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. The presence of citric acid promotes the ring-opening of glucose molecules, which further disintegrate into CQD by hydrothermal hydrolysis [20]. Fig. S1b shows the TGA of NCO and CQD after hydrothermal synthesis in an N2 environment. The NCO shows ∼25% of the weight loss between 200 and 300°C. It is attributed to the breakdown of organic species, mainly urea catalysts. Above 400°C the NCO stabilizes by eliminating organic and volatile species, resulting in pure nickel and cobalt oxides phases. In the case of CQD, the aromatization initiates with the increase in temperature. The continuous decrease in weight resembles pore recombination and removal of oxygen functionalities that promote the densification of the carbon matrix. Fig. 2 shows the TGA of ex-situ (sample after pyrolysis at 500°C) and in-situ composites (sample after hydrothermal synthesis) in N2. For ex-situ nanocomposites, the weight decreases with the increase in carbon content. The CQD starts the aromatization process as the temperature rises. In ex-situ samples, the NCO and CQD were hydrothermally prepared. Hence, even after the pyrolysis temperature of 500°C, the carbon present in the matrix starts post-densification from 300 to 400°C, resulting in weight loss, as depicted in Fig. 2a. However, an opposite trend is observed in-situ naocomposites, as shown in Fig. 2b. The composites with higher carbon precursors, i.e., glucose, show a minimum weight loss of 54%. As the glucose content decreases, the composite exhibits a weight loss of 66%. It can be attributed to the presence of urea as an NCO catalyst in the solution. After hydrothermal synthesis, the urea is combined with the glucose molecules. The amide bond breaks into smaller compounds with thermal energy in the N2 atmosphere, leading to weight loss. Hence, more urea in the solution leads to maximum weight loss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization of the composite electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM micrographs of NCO, where the hydrothermal synthesis of NCO leads to a nanowire morphology with an average wire diameter of 60 nm. The elemental mapping of NCO in Fig. S2 shows the homogeneous distribution of Ni, Co, and O atoms, indicating the successful synthesis of Ni-Co oxide. Fig. 3c–e shows the SEM images of ex-situ hybrid electrode morphology. It depicts that the NCO and CQD combined successfully during pyrolysis to form a composite. E:HE-1 in Fig. 3c shows uneven clusters of NCO and CQD; however, when NCO content increases to 50% in E:HE-2 (Fig. 3d), even mesopores sphere is formed, suggesting the clusters rearranged among themselves, forming a porous sphere via Ostwald ripening phenomena [21]. For E:HE-3 in Fig. 3e, the excess NCO nanowires get deposited on the carbon surface. Fig. 3f shows the SEM images of I:HE-1, where a hard composite sphere is formed without porosity. Carbon atoms dominate the sphere due to the high content of carbon precursors during synthesis. However, with the increase in NCO content in Fig. 3g–h, the NCO phases start nucleating along with carbon phases forming a nanocomposite. The NCO content is less for I:HE-2, which is indicated by incomplete flower-like morphology in Fig. 3g. For I:HE-3 in Fig. 3h, a homogeneous well-arranged flower petals-like morphology is obtained. Fig. 3i shows the magnified micrograph of I:HE-3, where the interconnected porous 3D architecture is observed. The SEM micrographs of ex-situ and in-situ hybrid composite electrodes suggest the dependence of composite properties on the synthesis mechanism. During ex-situ synthesis, the nanocomposite assembly takes place via pyrolysis. The hydrothermally synthesized NCO and CQD contain plenty of functionalities. On applying thermal energy in an inert atmosphere, NCO and CQD surface functionalities initiate the adhesion process. The TGA result also suggests a similar behavior as obtained from the SEM micrograph. For in-situ composites, the synthesis mechanism is complex. Here, the precursors and catalysts of NCO and CQD form resultant nanocomposites. The bottom-up approach shows that a homogeneous interconnected 3D nanoarchitecture composite is formed from NCO and CQD at an optimum precursor's ratio. The interconnected porous morphology facilitates an efficient diffusion pathway for the electrolytic ions.

Fig. 4 depicts the TEM micrograph of CQDs. Fig. 4a resembles the interconnected feature of CQD, having an average diameter of <10 nm. The magnified image in Fig. 4b suggests the successful formation of CQD where the core part contains sp3 hybridized carbon having an interplanar spacing of 0.33 nm, and the outer part with diffuse features resembles the defects due to oxygen functionalities. Fig. 4c displays the SAED pattern of CQD where a diffuse ring with diffraction spots is observed, indicating the formation of ordered carbon films. Fig. 4c shows the NCO nanowires with a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm corresponding to the (220) lattice plane [22]. The diffused SAED diffraction spots indicate the presence of oxygen vacancies. Figs. 4g-i resembles the TEM micrographs of ex-situ nanocomposites. The dark spots show the NCO particles residing on the carbon sheets. In ex-situ nanocomposites, the inhomogeneity in the NCO particles can be seen. Also, with the increase in NCO loading, the monoliths become dense, as seen in Fig. 4i. Fig. 4j–l shows the TEM micrographs of in-situ nanocomposites. Here at lower NCO precursor loading of 25%, only large carbon blocks can be seen, similar to the results obtained from SEM micrographs in Fig. 3f. Upon the increase in NCO content, in Figs. 4k and l dispersed NCO nanoparticles can be seen in the carbon film. However, Fig. 4l shows that I:HE-3 displays interconnected, homogeneous, and well-dispersed NCO nanoparticles observed in the carbon film. It indicates efficient charge storage electrode properties, where NCO and CQD synergistically improve electrochemical properties.

Raman spectroscopy was carried out to study the structure, disorder, and lattice deformation change in NCO and CQD hybrid composites. Fig. S3a illustrates the Raman spectroscopy of NCO and CQD. The two characteristic carbon peaks are observed at 1359 and 1617 cm-1, corresponding to the D and G bands [23]. It is worth noting that a large amount of active surface with oxygen functionalities broadens d-band and shifts the G-band from 1590 to 1617 cm−1. Nevertheless, the existence of the G-band that the carbon core contains an aromatic structure with sp3 hybridization. On the other hand, the three Raman optical phonon active bands observed at 217, 494, and 695 cm−1 depict the F2g, Eg, and A1g, respectively [24,25]. The peak originates due to the optical phonon confinement in Ni-Co oxide nanostructure. The presence of A1g mode depicts the stretching of oxygen atoms with Co3+ in tetrahedral sites. Eg vibrational mode attributes to the stretching of oxygen atoms at octahedral and tetrahedral sites. F2g vibrational modes arise from the triply degenerated modes from the octahedral sites [26]. Fig. 5 a illustrates the Raman spectroscopy of the NCOCQD nanocomposites. The spectra show the characteristic peak of Ni-Co oxides and carbon dots, where the peak intensities of both the phases strongly depend upon the composite ratio. The Raman spectra suggest the successful formation of NCO and CQD nanocomposites.

Further, the crystallographic phases of the hybrid composite were studied by XRD. Fig. S3b shows the XRD spectra of NCO and CQD. Six well-defined diffraction peaks were observed for NCO, which correspond to the inverse spinel structure of Ni-Co oxide. However, the XRD graph shows the broader peak with a small shift in the characteristic peak observed in the JCPDF card No. 20-0781 [12,27]. It is attributed to the change in morphology from bulk to nano-level. Also, the presence of oxygen vacancies in the NCO lattice distorted the nascent lattice structure of NCO. For CQD, the wide hump is observed at 23°, corresponding to the (002) planes. The widening of the carbon peak indicates the presence of nano-dimensional carbon. The XRD peaks of the hybrid composites are shown in Fig. 5b. Here, the mixture of NCO and CQD peaks are observed. The shift in characteristic peaks in the nanocomposite corresponds to oxygen vacancies in the matrix. The composites exhibit a small additional peak at 52°, corresponding to the (200) plane of Ni-carbide. The existence of the peak indicates the successful binding of the NCO phases with CQD. Also, I:HE-1 displays carbon type XRD peak, confirming the formation of carbon sphere with a minimal percentage of NCO, also depicted in the SEM micrograph (Fig. 3f). Figs. S3c and S4 demonstrate the sorption studies of NCO and nanocomposite electrodes, respectively. The NCO represents narrow pore size distribution with maximum pore volume at 2.5 nm (as shown in the inset of Fig. S3c), confirming the dominance of mesoporous structure of nanowires having BET surface area of 92 m2 g−1 with type IV isotherm plot. The porous nature of NCO nanowire enables strong bonding with the CQD during composite formation. The composite electrode shows similar type-IV isotherm plots with the dominance mesopores. However, the surface area increases with high carbon content in ex-situ samples (Fig. S4a–c) and decreases in-situ samples (Fig. S4d–f). Only thermal energy is employed during ex-situ composite synthesis, reducing surface functionalities and enabling adhesion among NCO and CQD. Also, the presence of mesopores in NCO acts as an active site for CQD for binding. In in-situ sample synthesis, hydrothermal and thermal energy are essential factors determining the nanocomposite properties. Here, the bonding between carbon and NCO precursor occurs at the nanoscale, where the pore gets retained after pyrolysis. Although the pore collapse during pyrolysis in excess carbon precursor for I:HE-1, promoting aromatization, thereby minimizing the surface area and porosity. Table 1 presents the physicochemical properties of CQD, NCO, and hybrid nanocomposites.

The elemental functionalities of the as-synthesized samples are studied through XPS. The survey spectra and the atomic element percentage are presented in Figs. 5c, S3d, and Table 1. The CQD illustrates two prominent peaks of C 1s and O 1s, indicating a high content of oxygen functionalities. The NCO depicts the presence of only Ni 2p, Co 2p, and O 1s elements, which suggests the purity of NCO. The hybrid electrode display C 1s, O 1s, Ni 2p, and Co 2p elements, representing the composite composition. Figs. 6 and S5 show the deconvoluted peaks of C 1s, O 1s, Ni 2p, and Co 2p samples. The C 1s spectra in Figs. 6a and S5a of CQD and nanocomposites suggest the presence of sp2 –CC-, sp3 –CO-, and –C=O functionalities that retain after the composite synthesis [23]. Also, the –C=O percentage increases in composites suggest carbon hybridization change from sp3 to sp2. The deconvoluted spectra of O 1s are displayed in Figs. 6b and S5b. The CQD display –C=O, quinone, and –COH hydroxyl functionalities, whereas NCO shows oxygen elements in oxide form [28]. Hydroxyl and quinone functionalities are observed due to trace carbon elements from urea. The ex-situ samples show two characteristics peak of oxygen corresponding to oxides and –C=O and quinone functionalities. However, different behavior is observed for in-situ composites. At I:HE-1, the oxide peak was absent. With the increase in the NCO precursors concentration, the prominent oxide peak is observed along with carbonyl, hydroxyl, and quinone functional groups. Figs. 6c-d and S5c-d show the Ni 2p and Co 2p deconvolution spectra. The two spin-orbit doublets with corresponding shakeup satellites are observed. Both Ni and Co are present in +2 and +3 oxidation states [29]. Although, the NCO characters for I:HE-1 are absent, suggesting the incomplete formation of NCO and CQD nanocomposite. Table S1 provides the peak assignment and the percentage of respective functionalities present in the samples.

The overall material characterization studies propose that the in-situ and ex-situ procedure shows changes in nanocomposite properties. Additionally, varying NCO and CQD content strongly affect the compositional and structural properties of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of the composite electrodes

ce:section.ce:para:
Three electrode studies emphasize the electrochemical properties of the composite as an electrode material for supercapacitor application. A potential range of -0.3–0.3 V was employed to study cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge curve (GCD) behavior. The electrochemical properties of NCO are displayed in Fig. S6. The CV curve in Fig. S6a shows the prominent redox peaks of Ni-Co oxide, which starts broadening at a higher scan rate. Similarly, in the GCD plot (Fig. S6b), a plateau is observed at ∼0.1 V. Here, the redox process occurs in the mixed metal oxides where Ni and Co readily change their oxidation state, providing pseudocapacitance. The redox pairs Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ undergo reversible redox reactions that provide pseudocapacitance. The possible reduction-oxidation reaction occurring during the electrochemical process is summarized as follows [30]:

Further, Fig. S1a depicts the significance of proper performance metrics analysis to obtain an accurate electrochemical property of the electrode. Fig. S6c shows the specific capacitance of NCO at various current densities. Conventionally, NCO delivers a specific capacitance of 2311 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 using Eq. (S2). By considering the discharge area of NCO, the true specific capacitance is 2477 F g−1 using Eq. (S3). Although, the maximum capacitance delivered by NCO is 3114 F g−1 at a current density of 3 A g−1. Generally, in EDLC-type electrodes, the capacitance decreases with increasing current density. This relation might not hold for pseudocapacitors, as redox processes require a specific potential and optimum current to obtain the best electrode performance [31]. Fig. S7a–f shows the CV curves of the nanocomposite electrodes at different scan rates where the presence of noticeable redox pairs indicates the effective contribution of the NCO. Fig. 7 a illustrates the comparative CV curve of all the composites at 0.5 mV s−1. Here, I:HE-3 shows the largest enclosed area. The broad shape of the CV curve indicates that capacitive and Faradaic mechanisms store the electrode charge. The synergistic effect of carbon species and NCO species can be seen. The high surface area and mesoporous structure of NCO undergo a redox reaction, and the efficient carbon conducting pathway quickly transfers the electron to the external circuit. The presence of oxygen vacancies in NCO phase eases the ion transport mechanism in the electrode. The other electrode also offers the synergistic charge storage behavior. However, the overall performance depends upon the morphology for electrode-electrolyte interaction, conductivity, pore size distribution, and surface area. Fig. S8a–f displays the GCD plot of composites. Here, the distorted curve indicates the influence of reversible redox reactions occurring during charging and discharging. A comparative GCD plot at 1 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 7b. I:HE-3 delivers the highest discharge time among other composites. Fig. 7c shows the specific capacitance calculated from the conventional approach using Eq. (S2). The I:HE-3 offers a specific capacitance of 4362 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Although the discharge area is considered instead of discharge time, the true specific capacitance is 3802 F g−1, As presented in Fig. 7d. The performance analysis by conventional equation provides the overestimated performance. Hence, Eq. (S3) delivers the true interpretation of the nanocomposites. The specific capacitance of E:HE-1, E:HE-2, E:HE-3, I:HE-1, and I:HE-2 are 193, 1365, 1420, 614, and 902 F g−1 a current density of 1 A g−1, respectively. The ex-situ composite shows ∼7 orders of performance improvement from the use of 25% to 50% of NCO. After that, the capacitance increases to 4% at 75% NCO. Encouragingly, the capacitance increases to 30% for in-situ composites until the NCO precursors are 50%, with further increase in NCO precursor to 75%, capacitance drastically enhanced to 70%. It suggests that the electrochemical properties are strongly related to morphology, surface area, and the diffusion pathway of electroactive materials. Further, I:HE-3 nanocomposite was tested with a higher mass loading of 5 mg cm−2. Fig. S9a and b show the CV and GCD curves of the electrode at different scan rates and current densities, respectively. The CV curve shows the dominant redox peaks and nonlinear GCD profiles. Fig. S9c depicts the comparative specific capacitance of I:HE-3 at 1 and 5 mg cm−2. Here, the nanocomposite delivers 2824 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 retaining the 75% capacitance compared with the electrode performance at a mass loading of 1 mg cm−2. The minimal decrease in performance is due to the restricted movement of electrolyte ions and increased contact resistance at higher mass loading. Coming to the cyclic stability of the I:HE-3, Fig. S9d displays the stability of the electrode till 4000 charge-discharge cycles. The electrode shows 84% capacitance retention implying fully reversible charge-discharge behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Asymmetric device studies of the composite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The asymmetric device shows a wider electrochemical stable potential window than symmetric devices. Using keratin fiber-derived hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) as a negative electrode provides multifold advantages. The synthesis procedure of HPC is facile, and low cost as waste human hairs are utilized as a precursor. The three-electrode electrochemical performances of HPC are provided in Fig. S10a–d, where HPC shows excellent electrochemical performance displaying true capacitance of 900 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. However, the performance reported is in a three-electrode system, which is a half-cell configuration. In device configuration, the HPC will not show the remarkable performance we are trying to achieve. The reason is the surface dominant charge storage mechanism, where the exposed surface takes part in the charge storage that results dead electrode volume. It decreases the specific performance of the device. In contrary, in pseudocapacitance, whole bulk electrodes participate in the charge storage process. Hence, there is a need for a hybrid electrode consisting of pseudocapacitive materials. Hence, the study utilizes the high performance HPC as a negative electrode in asymmetric supercapacitor. .Accordingly, the mass balance ratio (M +/M-) of composites and HPC were calculated using Eq. (S4) is presented in Fig. S10e. HPC acts as a negative electrode material, whereas NCO and its nanocomposites are positive electrodes. When combined in an asymmetric configuration, the device widens its working potential window from 0 to 1.5 V for NCO to 0–1.6 V for the nanocomposites.

Fig. S11 depicts the asymmetric device performance of NCO//HPC. The device widens its working voltage from 0 to 1.5 V in an aqueous electrolyte 6 M KOH. The CV curve in Fig. S11a shows the combination of capacitive and Faradaic behavior. After 1 V, a sharp increase in the current is observed, indicating the pseudocapacitive contribution of NCO. However, no prominent peak is present due to the nanostructure effect of NCO. As the size of the pseudocapacitive material decreases, the surface activity increases. The presence of large surface active sites for electrolyte ion interaction results in broad and diffuse peaks enhancing the rate capability of the electrode. The nanowire with an average diameter of 60 nm provides many active surface sites undergoing charge-storage processes. Fig. S11b shows the GCD profile of NCO//HPC where a nonlinear charge-discharge curve is observed, indicating the influence of NCO. The true specific capacitance of 75 F g−1 is achieved at a current density of 1 A g−1, as depicted in Fig. S11c. The Ragone plot in Fig. S11d shows the device delivers an energy density of 20 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 500 W kg−1. The energy density of the device gets enhanced due to asymmetric assembly. However, the poor conductivity of NCO restricts electrolyte ion and electron diffusion pathways through the electrode, reducing the device's performance. To eliminate the shortcomings of poor conductivity and stability. NCO are combined with CQD via in-situ and ex-situ processes. The section discusses asymmetric devices based on as-synthesized nanocomposites as the positive electrode and HPC as the negative electrode.

Fig. S12a-f displays the CV curves of the asymmetric hybrid devices at the different operating potential windows at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The device shows the combination of reversible Faradaic and capacitive behavior till 1.6 V. Beyond 1.6 V, the initiation of an anodic peak occurs due to the decomposition of water, which is detrimental to the device. Hence, the potential window of 0–1.6 V was used to study the electrochemical performances. Fig. 8 a shows the comparative CV graph of hybrid electrode devices. The CV curves of asymmetric hybrid devices at different scan rates are presented in Fig. S13a–e. E:HE-1 and I:HE-1 based devices show a flat CV profile, with the increase in NCO species, the E:HE-2 and I:HE-2 illustrate the increase in CV area. The best performance was achieved at the maximum NCO content for E:HE-3 and I:HE-3. Here, the composite synthesized via an in-situ mechanism with the highest NCO species (I:HE-3) shows superior performance among other nanocomposites. The CV curves at different scan rates of I:HE-3 have presented in Fig. 8b. The curve shows a quasi-linear charge storage process till 0.8 V, indicating the dominance of capacitive behavior. After 0.8 V, the sharp increase in current density depicts the supremacy of the Faradaic mechanism. The large enclosed area from 0.8 to 1.6 V suggests the effective participation of the nanocomposite, which retain even at a high scan rate, indicating excellent rate capability. The highest charge-discharge time was obtained for I:HE-3, as shown in the comparative GCD profile in Fig. 8c. Fig. S14a–e shows the GCD profile of ex-situ composites, I:HE-1 and I:HE-2, at different current densities. The distorted GCD plot indicates the presence of a reversible redox reaction. The GCD profile of I:HE-3 in Fig. 8d shows mixed charge storage behavior where charges are stored by bilayer formation and electron transfer mechanism. It is worth noting that I:HE-3 shows a diffuse GCD peak due to nanoarchitecture morphology which provides large surface active sites. The bulk NCO shows a plateau-type discharge profile. With the decrease in particle size to nano-dimension, the surface becomes more involved than the bulk, resulting in diffuse and enhanced charge storage behavior. Fig. 8e shows the true specific capacitance of the hybrid devices in different current densities. The true capacitance calculated using the Eq. (S10) shows that I:HE-3 delivers the device-specific capacitance of 292 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with 30% retention till 20 A g−1. The other composites show the specific capacitance of 10, 109, 15, 117, and 182 F g−1 for I:HE-1, I:HE-2, E:HE-1, E:HE-2, and E:HE-3, respectively. The I:HE-3 shows a moderate coulombic efficiency (Fig. S15a) above 60%, indicating minimum loss during the charge-discharge process. The iR drop of the nanocomposites is presented in Fig. S15b. The I:HE-3 shows the minimal voltage drop among other composites. The iR profile suggests that the presence of CQD pockets throughout the NCO phases acts as a bridge that ensures the optimum conductivity of the electrode material. Also, the inert behavior of the CQD provides mechanical support to the electrode, enhancing the hybrid electrode stability. The Ragone plot of the composites is shown in Fig. 8f. The I:HE-3 delivers the maximum device-specific energy density of 104 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1 that retains till 30 Wh kg−1 at 20 kW kg−1. The electrochemical parameters are calculated using the true analysis method using Eqs. (S8)–(S11) to avoid discrepancies for over-or underestimation of the electrochemical performance. However, the parameters have also been computed using conventional Eqs. (S5)–(S7) for literature comparison, and the graph is presented as Fig. S15c-d. Table 2 compares the performance of the as-assembled asymmetric device with the reported literature.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy has studied the frequency response behavior of I:HE-3//AC. Fig. 9 a shows the Nyquist plot of the asymmetric device. The inset of Fig. 9a shows the equivalent circuit obtains from fitting the Nyquist plot. The Nyquist data was fitted using Nova 1.1 software. The equivalent circuit consists of series resistance Rs, charge transfer resistance Rct, Warburg impedance W, and two constant phase elements (CPE-1 and CPE-2). CPE element represents the capacitance of the device. The device delivers minimum Rs, Rct, and W values of 0.5,4.7, 13.3 Ω cm−2, respectively. The CPE-1 with 52.5 μF cm−2 and CPE-2 with 430.2 mF cm−2. Fig. 9b shows the Bode phase of an asymmetric device where the phase angle reaches 45°. It indicates that the device undergoes electrostatic and Faradaic type charge storage processes.

The excellent charge storage behavior of I:HE-3 can be explained by Fig. 6c-d. Fig. 9c depicts the I:HE-3 morphology schematic where the presence of nanopetals permits a large amount of electrolyte ions, thereby improving ion diffusivity. Fig. 9d suggests the importance of oxygen vacancies in the octahedral and tetrahedral voids in Ni-Co oxide phases. The oxygen deficiency allows the accessibility of cations (Ni and Co) to undergo Faradaic processes. It enhances the conductivity and improves the electrochemical kinetics of I:HE-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of I:He-3//HPC device in redox-active electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: The above studies suggest that I:HE-3//HPC delivers the best performance among the hybrid electrode nanocomposites. The present section provides an additional approach to enhance the performance of the supercapacitor. The addition of redox mediators in the traditional electrolyte can significantly improve the electrochemical behavior of the device. Here two different redox-active electrolytes (a) potassium iodide (KI) and (b) hydroxyquinone (HQ) are added to 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte to study the electrochemical performances. The choice of KI and HQ was made to study the charge storage mechanism of ionic and organic redox mediators in 6 M KOH. The electrochemical properties tested in 0.02 M of redox electrolyte mediator are (a) to minimize the mass of KI/HQ: At a high KI/HQ concentration, the mass of these compound need to be added to the electrode mass. Since they are also contributing to charge storage, and (b) At a higher concentration of KI, negatively charged iodides were more electrostatically attracted to one another due to the excess concentration, which decreases the overall ionic conductivity. Fig. 10 a shows the comparative CV loop of I:HE-3//HPC in (a) 6 M KOH, (b) 6 M KOH + 0.02 M KI, and (c) 6 M KOH+0.02 M HQ redox-active electrolytes at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The CV curve indicates a quasi-rectangular behavior with the introduction of KI in the device. In the presence of HQ, the performance deteriorates compared to the conventional 6 M KOH electrolyte. It suggests that the charge storage mechanism and electron transfer pathway are vital in determining the device's performance. The decrease in device performance in the HQ environment can be attributed to the size of the organic compound and the presence of a high concentration of K + and OH− species that undergoes sluggish ions transportation decreasing the overall performance. Also, the mismatch between the electrode pore size and HQ reduces the electrode-electrolyte interaction [40]. On the contrary, an interesting phenomenon can be seen by introducing KI species in I:HE-3//HPC devices. The CV curve shows the dominance of capacitive charge storage with near rectangular behavior. Here, the anionic species, I − ions, actively participate in the charge storage process, enhancing overall device performance. Unlike other Faradaic processes, the electrochemical oxidation reaction of iodide delivers both negatively charged reactant and product, making this process unique. It implies that the reactant I − and product In− can serve as charge balancing anions promoting the bilayer formation at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Also, during the charge-discharge process, the iodide ions under redox couple recombination of I3−/I −, I2−/I −, I2−/I3− toward I5−, thereby contributing Faradaic charge-storage process [41]. Hence, the increased charge storage capacity is due to an anionic reactant and product that undergoes electron transfer and electrosorption. The comparative GCD curve in Fig. 10b has a similar behavior of redox-active electrolytes at a current density of 1 A g−1. A significant improvement in the charge-discharge time can be seen in KI-based device. In the absence of redox-mediated electrolyte, the device completes its single GCD cycle in 800 s. With the addition of 0.02 M KI species, the device completes one GCD cycle in 1375s. Further, Fig. 10c shows the CV curve of the I:HE-3-KI device at different potential ranges at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Till 1.6 V, no anodic peak is observed, suggesting that the device has a stable working potential window of 0–1.6 V. Fig. S16a shows the CV curve for I:HE-3-HQ device in the different potential ranges at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Fig. 10d-e shows the CV and GCD curves of the I:HE-3-KI device at various scan rates and current density, respectively. The CV curve shows the expansion in the CV loop, depicting the enormous charge storage capacity. Also, with the increase in sweep rate, the curve maintains its shape, indicating the excellent rate capability of the device. Similarly, the GCD curve shows the mixture of capacitive and non-capacitive charge storage processes with a long charge-discharge time. Fig. 10f shows the accurate, specific capacitance of the redox-active devices at different current densities, calculated by the Eq. (S10). The CV and GCD curves of I:HE-3-HQ device are presented in Fig. S16b and c. The I:HE-3-KI device offers an excellent specific capacitance of 463 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The achieved capacitance is ∼37% higher than the conventional electrolyte-based device. The I:HE-3-HQ device depict the detrimental performance suggesting the incompatibility between electrode and electrolyte ions. Fig. S16d shows the accurate coulombic efficiency of the devices calculated from Eq. (S11). The I:HE-3-KI device maintain the coulombic efficiency above 60% even at a high current density attributing to a superior charge-discharge process. Fig. S16e shows the iR drop profile of the devices with different current densities. The I:HE-3-HQ device has strong resistive properties, which is the primary reason behind the detrimental performance. The I:HE-3-KI device shows minimal resistive characteristics that suggest the reason behind the superior electrochemical performance.

Further, the performance of the devices has been studied by the Ragone plot depicted in Fig. 11 a. Here, the I:HE-3-KI device show the accurate energy density of 165 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1 that retains till 50 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 25 kW kg−1. Here, a noticeable decrease in the energy density is observed for I:HE-3-HQ device indicating the inefficient charge-storage process. The stability of the device over repeated charge-discharge cycles is one of the critical parameters for designing a high-performance supercapacitor. Fig. 11b depicts the capacitance retention of the devices in a conventional and redox-active electrolyte (KI) till 10,000 continuous GCD cycles at a current density of 15 Ag−1. The I:HE-3-KI device delivered 87%, and I:HE-3 device showed 84% performance retention indicating excellent stability. The GCD curve at 1st, 5000th and last cycle for I:HE-3//AC in 6 M KOH and 6 M KOH+0.02 M KI are presented in Figs. S17a and b, respectively. It is worth noting that adding a small amount of KI as redox-active species has significantly improved the device performance without compromising the stability parameter. Fig. 11c shows the CV loop at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 of two I:HE-3-KI devices in series and parallel combination. The CV loop doubles its area in parallel combination and widens its potential in a series arrangement, indicating compatibility of the device with the external circuit. Fig. 11d shows the digital photograph of the cell oriented in series and parallel combinations. Fig. 11e illustrates the digital picture of red, green, and blue LEDs illumination from the I:HE-3-KI device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of a flexible redox-active gel polymer electrolyte based I:HE-3//HPC device
ce:section.ce:para: Advancements in portable, lightweight, and wearable electronic gadgets demand flexible charge storage devices. The present section represents an approach to designing a low-cost, flexible asymmetric device using I:HE-3 and HPC as positive and negative electrodes, respectively. A redox-active gel polymer (KOH+KI+PVA) is used as a separator and current collector. Fig. 12 a shows the CV curve of an as-assembled flexible device at various sweep rates. A hump at 0.3 V indicates the Faradaic charge-storage process. The curve maintains a near rectangular behavior suggesting a capacitive charge storage process. Fig. 12b shows the GCD profile at different current densities. The GCD curve show deviation from the linear behavior due to the Faradaic charge storage processes. The true specific capacitance of 192 mF cm−1 is achieved at a current density of 1 mA cm−1. Fig. 12c indicates the discrepancies regarding the conventional and true evaluation procedure, where an overestimated performance of the device is observed by practicing conventional equations. The Ragone plot, as presented in Fig. 12d, shows that the device delivers an excellent true energy density of 38 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1. The flexible study of the device was carried out at different bending angles. A CV graph at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for the same is presented in Fig. 12e. The performance retention at a different bending angle is shown in Fig. 12f. The device increases the performance at a low bending angle, attributing to the electrode and gel polymer electrolyte rearrangement. However, the device does not exhibit deterioration in the performance at a bending angle, indicating a proper adhesion among the components of a flexible supercapacitor.

The advancement in a supercapacitor field demands a hybrid mechanism, where the material and electrochemical properties can be tuned according to the demand. The current study utilizes a cost-effective and facile approach to synthesizing a hybrid electrode for a supercapacitor. The nanocomposite I:HE-3 displays a homogeneous nanoflower-like morphology promoting efficient charge storage capacity, with a high surface area of 97 m2 g−1. Oxygen vacancies provide resistance-free ion mobility by expanding the lattice size. However, the determination of electrochemical properties is an essential objective. This study eliminated the discrepancies in the performance estimation where dV(voltage)/dt(time) is considered constant, which does not hold true for the hybrid system. The hybrid systems performance has been studied using true analysis, where the electrode I:HE-3 delivers excellent specific capacitance of 3802 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The achieved capacitance is much higher than NCO, indicating improved electrochemical properties by adding CQD. Further, in device configuration, the system shows a specific capacitance of 292 F g−1, upon adding redox-mediated electrolyte KI the specific capacitance increases to 464 F g−1. It happens due to the unique charge storage capacity of iodide ions, which promote bilayer formation and direct electron transfer. The maximum energy density achieved is 165 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1, with exceptional device stability of 87% until 10,000 GCD cycles. The electrode shows good compatibility in a flexible design, delivering a specific capacitance of 192 mF cm−1 at a current density of 1 mA cm−1 and energy density of 38 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1. The comprehensive study suggests an assembly of a low-cost and high-performance supercapacitor by tailoring its electrode and electrolyte components.

Prerna Sinha: Data curation, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Kamal K. Kar: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.
